title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion for Hybrid Search
concept: Merging two ranked lists by rank position instead of raw score, sidestepping the normalization problem.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, how did we combine sparse and dense search results using a Weighted Linear Combination?

options.text: It completely ignores the sparse search results.
It uses a weighted parameter (alpha) to blend scaled sparse and dense scores together.
It only looks at the rank position and ignores raw scores entirely.

exercises.explain: Weighted Linear Combination uses a tuning parameter (often called alpha) to balance how much weight to give to the sparse (keyword) score versus the dense (vector) score.

cards.title: Comparing Apples to Oranges
cards.body: Raw scores from keyword search (like BM25) can be any number from 0 to 100+, while vector scores are often decimals between -1 and 1. How do we merge them fairly without writing complex normalization math that breaks every time our data changes?

cards.title: What is Reciprocal Rank Fusion?
cards.body: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is a method that combines multiple search result lists by looking only at the rank (position) of each item. It scores each item by adding up the inverse of its ranks, meaning items that appear near the top of either list get the highest final score.

cards.title: The Leaderboard Shortcut
cards.body: Imagine trying to merge a list of the 'top foods' ranked by price and another ranked by spiciness. Instead of converting dollars to heat levels, you just look at their leaderboard positions: 1st place gets the most points, 2nd place gets slightly less, and so on.

cards.title: RRF in Action
cards.body: If a document is ranked 2nd in keyword search and 5th in vector search, RRF calculates its score using the formula 1/(2 + k) + 1/(5 + k), where k is a small constant (like 60) to prevent top ranks from dominating too heavily.

cards.title: Myth: RRF Has Zero Parameters
cards.body: While RRF works incredibly well out of the box without tuning weights, it actually has one hyperparameter 'k' (usually set to 60). This constant ensures that a rank of 1st vs 2nd doesn't completely overwhelm a document that ranked 3rd on both lists.

cards.title: Raw Scores vs. Ranks
cards.body: RRF discards the messy, incompatible raw scores and standardizes everything into simple rank positions before combining them.

left.text: Needs complex math to scale 0.85 (cosine) and 18.4 (BM25) to the same range.

right.text: Merges 1st place and 3rd place directly using rank formulas.

exercises.prompt: In the RRF formula, why does a document appearing at the very top of a list get a higher score?

options.text: A higher rank number (like 100th) gives a larger score contribution.
A lower rank number (like 1st) gives a larger score contribution because it is in the denominator.
The raw similarity score is multiplied by the rank.
Documents are sorted alphabetically to break ties.

exercises.explain: Because RRF uses the reciprocal of the rank (1/rank), a lower rank number (like 1st place) yields a much larger value than a higher rank number (like 100th place), pushing top-ranked items to the front.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Reciprocal Rank Fusion allows you to merge results without normalizing the raw scores of the sparse and dense search engines.
exercises.explain: This is true. Because RRF only looks at the position (1st, 2nd, 3rd...) of the results, the actual scale of the raw scores is completely ignored, solving the normalization problem.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about how RRF evaluates documents:
exercises.sentence: Instead of using raw scores, Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges documents based entirely on their rank position in each list.
exercises.explain: RRF only cares about the order (rank position) of the results, completely ignoring the raw numeric scores calculated by the database.

exercises.prompt: Identify which search scoring calculation was generated by an AI applying Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF).

options.text: Document A is 1st in keyword search and 3rd in vector search. Using RRF with k=60, its final score is 1/(1+60) + 1/(3+60) = 0.0322.
Document A has a BM25 score of 12.5 and a vector similarity of 0.82. We normalize them to a 0-1 scale and multiply by a weight of 0.7.
Document A is kept only if its keyword score is above 10.0 and its vector score is above 0.80, throwing away the rest.

exercises.explain: The first option uses RRF logic by converting the positions (1st and 3rd) into reciprocal scores (1/(1+60) and 1/(3+60)) to calculate the combined score, which is the signature method of Reciprocal Rank Fusion.

exercises.prompt: Why is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) often preferred in production over Weighted Linear Combination when combining search results from completely different systems?

options.text: RRF does not require normalizing or scaling the raw scores from different search engines before merging them.
RRF requires finding an exact decimal weight (alpha) between 0 and 1 for every database update.
RRF only works if both search engines use the exact same vector distance metric.
RRF simply adds the raw BM25 score directly to the cosine similarity score.

exercises.explain: RRF uses rank positions ($1 / (\text{rank} + k)$) rather than raw scores. This entirely bypasses the need to scale or normalize raw BM25 scores (which can be unbounded) and vector similarities (which might be cosine or L2), making it highly robust.
